expectations.text: DEAL VELOCITY: DEAL HEALTH ASSESSMENT classifies this as AT RISK (not HEALTHY despite 22 months remaining) with reasoning connecting at least three of: 84%-to-61% adoption drop, the P1 SLA breach (9 hours vs 4-hour SLA), Rachel Dunn's disengagement from the last 3 check-ins, the new CFO's vendor audit of contracts over $500K, and competitor ad targeting -- so the account team treats this as a save situation, not a routine QBR
expectations.evidence: Classified as 'AT RISK' with explicit statement: 'This is not a routine QBR. This is a save situation.' Five reasoning points: 1. 'Adoption decline: Platform adoption dropped from 84% to 61% over two quarters -- a 23-percentage-point decline,' 2. 'SLA breach: P1 support incident in November took 9 hours to resolve against a 4-hour SLA -- a 125% overshoot,' 3. 'Executive disengagement: CISO Rachel Dunn has not attended the last 3 monthly check-ins,' 4. 'New CFO vendor audit: James Whitfield is auditing all vendor contracts over $500K,' 5. 'Competitor activity: Sentinel Shield and CyberArch are running LinkedIn ads targeting Meridian.' All five factors connected.

expectations.text: WIN RATE: KNOWN VS. UNKNOWN separates confirmed facts (adoption numbers, SLA breach details, stakeholder map, renewal timeline, competitor ad targeting) from critical unknowns (root cause of adoption decline, Rachel's current sentiment, James Whitfield's evaluation criteria and stance, whether competitors have made direct contact beyond ads) -- giving the rep a checklist of intelligence gaps to close during the QBR itself
expectations.evidence: KNOWN section covers: contract value and term, adoption drop (84% to 61%), P1 incident (9-hour resolution vs 4-hour SLA), Rachel Dunn absent 3 check-ins, Marcus Tillman active, James Whitfield auditing contracts >$500K, competitor LinkedIn ads. UNKNOWN section structured as a table with: 'Root cause of adoption decline,' 'Rachel Dunn's current sentiment,' 'James Whitfield's evaluation criteria,' 'James Whitfield's stance on this contract,' 'Whether competitors have made direct contact,' 'Whether internal budget pressure exists.' Each unknown includes 'How to Uncover' guidance for the QBR.

expectations.text: WIN RATE: RISK FACTORS identifies 2-3 threats grounded in the deal details -- such as the new CFO using the SLA miss and declining adoption as justification to renegotiate or RFP, Rachel's silence indicating she may no longer be championing internally, or competitor outreach converting interest while adoption declines -- each risk the rep can proactively address rather than react to
expectations.evidence: Three deal-specific risk factors: 'Risk 1: CFO Uses SLA Miss + Adoption Decline as RFP Justification' -- 'he has everything he needs to justify an RFP or renegotiation. The data tells a story of declining value.' 'Risk 2: Rachel's Silence Signals Internal Champion Loss' -- '3 consecutive missed check-ins is not busy -- it's disengagement' with four possible explanations. 'Risk 3: Competitor Displacement While Adoption Declines' -- 'users who are not on the platform are users who would not notice a switch.' Each includes a mitigation strategy.

expectations.text: SALES CYCLE COMPRESSION: RECOMMENDED NEXT ACTION provides a specific QBR strategy -- such as leading with an adoption recovery plan before James raises it, requesting a 1:1 with Rachel before the QBR to re-establish executive alignment, or preparing a value realization report quantifying ROI in terms the CFO cares about -- not 'present a business review' but a concrete play designed to neutralize the biggest risk before it becomes a formal objection
expectations.evidence: Detailed before/during QBR strategy: Before: 'Request a 1:1 with Rachel Dunn' with specific framing, 'Prepare a Value Realization Report for James Whitfield' with structure (ROI quantification, adoption recovery plan, SLA remediation, competitive positioning). During: 'Lead with the adoption recovery plan -- Do not wait for James to raise adoption decline,' 'Address the P1 SLA breach directly,' 'Close intelligence gaps' with specific questions for each stakeholder. Full proposed QBR agenda included with time allocations.

expectations.text: DEAL VELOCITY: NEXT STEP TO BOOK identifies one specific post-QBR action (e.g., a dedicated session with James Whitfield to walk through contract value quantification, or an adoption workshop with Marcus's team to reverse the usage decline) -- a concrete next meeting that locks in momentum from the QBR rather than letting the account drift for another quarter
expectations.evidence: Two specific post-QBR actions: '1. A dedicated 45-minute session with James Whitfield within 2 weeks of the QBR. Purpose: walk through the full contract value quantification in CFO terms (cost per incident prevented, ROI on security investment, total cost of ownership vs. alternatives).' '2. An adoption workshop with Marcus Tillman's team within 1 week of the QBR. Purpose: diagnose the root causes of the 84% to 61% decline, identify the teams/users who dropped off, and build a 90-day adoption recovery plan.' Final note: 'Both meetings lock in momentum from the QBR and prevent the account from drifting for another quarter.'

expectations.text: WIN RATE: The brief arms the rep for the CFO conversation specifically -- anticipating that James Whitfield will evaluate the $890K contract through a cost-reduction lens and preparing the rep with ROI framing, adoption recovery timeline, and SLA remediation evidence before they walk into a room with someone they've never met who controls budget
expectations.evidence: Dedicated section 'PREPARING FOR THE CFO CONVERSATION' states: 'James Whitfield will evaluate the $890K contract through a cost-reduction or value-justification lens. He has no relationship history with us.' Includes a table of 5 likely questions with prepared responses: 'What are we getting for $890K?' (Value Realization Report), 'Why has adoption dropped?' (Adoption recovery plan), 'What happened with the SLA breach?' (SLA remediation evidence), 'How does your pricing compare to alternatives?' (Total cost of ownership), 'Can we renegotiate the contract?' (prepared position on what can be offered). Also includes competitor-specific talk tracks for Sentinel Shield and CyberArch.
